title: Imperante Augusto Natus Est--
content:
آ آ آ آ What it was struck the terror into me?
آ آ آ آ This, Publius: closer! while we wait our turn
آ آ آ آ Iï؟½ll tell you. Waterï؟½s warm (they ring inside)
آ آ آ آ At the eighth hour, till when no use to bathe.

آ آ آ آ Here in the vestibule where now we sit,
آ آ آ آ One scarce stood yesterday, the throng was such
آ آ آ آ Of loyal gapers, folk all eye and ear
آ آ آ آ While Lucius Varius Rufus in their midst
آ آ آ آ Read out that long-planned late-completed piece,
آ آ آ آ His Panegyric on the Emperor.
آ آ آ آ ï؟½Nobody like him,ï؟½ little Flaccus laughed,
آ آ آ آ ï؟½At leading forth an Epos with due pomp!
آ آ آ آ Only, when godlike Cï؟½sar swells the theme,
آ آ آ آ How should mere mortals hope to praise aright?
آ آ آ آ Tell me, thou offshoot of Etruscan kings!ï؟½
آ آ آ آ Whereat Mï؟½cenas smiling sighed assent.
آ آ آ آ I paid my quadrans, left the Thermï؟½ roar
آ آ آ آ Of rapture as the poet asked: ï؟½What place
آ آ آ آ Among the godships Jove, for Cï؟½sarï؟½s sake,
آ آ آ آ Would bid its actual occupant vacate
آ آ آ آ In favor of the new divinity?ï؟½
آ آ آ آ And got the expected answer, ï؟½Yield thine own!ï؟½
آ آ آ آ Jove thus dethroned, I somehow wanted air,
آ آ آ آ And found myself a-pacing street and street,
آ آ آ آ Letting the sunset, rosy over Rome,
آ آ آ آ Clear my head dizzy with the hubbub, say,
آ آ آ آ As if thoughtï؟½s dance therein had kicked up dust
آ آ آ آ By trampling on all else: the world lay prone,
آ آ آ آ As, poet-propped, in brave hexameters,
آ آ آ آ Their subject triumphed up from man to God.
آ آ آ آ Caius Octavius Cï؟½sar the August,
آ آ آ آ Where was escape from his prepotency?
آ آ آ آ I judge I may have passed, how many piles
آ آ آ آ Of structure dropt like doles from his free hand
آ آ آ آ To Rome on every side? Why, right and left,
آ آ آ آ For temples youï؟½ve the Thundering Jupiter,
آ آ آ آ Avenging Mars, Apollo Palatine:
آ آ آ آ How count Piazza, Forum, thereï؟½s a third
آ آ آ آ All but completed. Youï؟½ve the Theatre
آ آ آ آ Named of Marcellus, all his work, such work!
آ آ آ آ One thought still ending, dominating all,
آ آ آ آ With warrant Varius sang, ï؟½Be Cï؟½sar God!ï؟½
آ آ آ آ By what a hold arrests he Fortuneï؟½s wheel,
آ آ آ آ Obtaining and retaining heaven and earth
آ آ آ آ Through Fortune, if you like, but favor, no!
آ آ آ آ For the great deeds flashed by me, fast and thick
آ آ آ آ As stars which storm the sky on autumn nights,
آ آ آ آ Those conquests! but peace crowned them, so, of peace
آ آ آ آ Count up his titles only, these, in few,
آ آ آ آ Ten years Triumvir, Consul thirteen times,
آ آ آ آ Emperor, nay, the glory topping all,
آ آ آ آ Hailed Father of his Country, last and best
آ آ آ آ Of titles, by himself accepted so:
آ آ آ آ And why not? See but feats achieved in Rome,
آ آ آ آ Not to say, Italy, he planted there
آ آ آ آ Some thirty colonies, but Rome itself
آ آ آ آ All new-built, ï؟½marble now, brick once,ï؟½ he boasts:
آ آ آ آ This Portico, that Circus. Would you sail?
آ آ آ آ He has drained Tiber for you: would you walk?
آ آ آ آ He straightened out the long Flaminian Way.
آ آ آ آ Poor? Profit by his score of donatives?
آ آ آ آ Rich, that is, mirthful? Half-a-hundred games
آ آ آ آ Challenge your choice! Thereï؟½s Rome, for you and me
آ آ آ آ Only? The centre of the world besides!
آ آ آ آ For, look the wide world over, where ends Rome?
آ آ آ آ To sunrise? Thereï؟½s Euphrates, all between!
آ آ آ آ To sunset? Ocean and immensity:
آ آ آ آ North, stare till Danube stops you: South, see Nile,
آ آ آ آ The Desert and the earth-upholding Mount.
آ آ آ آ Well may the poet-people each with each
آ آ آ آ Vie in his praise, our company of swans,
آ آ آ آ Virgil and Horace, singers, in their way,
آ آ آ آ Nearly as good as Varius, though less famed:
آ آ آ آ Well may they cry, ï؟½No mortal, plainly God!ï؟½

آ آ آ آ Thus to myself myself said, while I walked:
آ آ آ آ Or would have said, could thought attain to speech,
آ آ آ آ Clean baffled by enormity of bliss
آ آ آ آ The while I strove to scale its heights and sound
آ آ آ آ Its depths, this Amsterdam oï؟½er all the world
آ آ آ آ Of one who was but born, like you, like me,
آ آ آ آ Like all the world he owns, of flesh and blood.
آ آ آ آ But he, how grasp, how gauge his own conceit
آ آ آ آ Of bliss to me near inconceivable?
آ آ آ آ Or, since such flight too much makes reel the brain,
آ آ آ آ Letï؟½s sink, and so take refuge, as it were,
آ آ آ آ From lifeï؟½s excessive altitude, to liftï؟½s
آ آ آ آ Breathable wayside shelter at its base!
آ آ آ آ If blooms thus large this Cï؟½sar to myself
آ آ آ آ Of senatorial rank and somebody,
آ آ آ آ How must he strike the vulgar nameless crowd,
آ آ آ آ In numerous swarm thatï؟½s nobody at all?
آ آ آ آ Why, for an instance, much as yon gold shape
آ آ آ آ Crowned, sceptred, on the temple opposite,
آ آ آ آ Fulgurant Jupiter, must daze the sense
آ آ آ آ Of, say, yon outcast begging from its step!
آ آ آ آ ï؟½What, Anti-Cï؟½sar, monarch in the mud,
آ آ آ آ As he is pinnacled above thy pate?
آ آ آ آ Ay, beg away! thy lot contrasts full well
آ آ آ آ With his whose bounty yields thee this support,
آ آ آ آ Our Holy and Inviolable One,
آ آ آ آ Cï؟½sar, whose bounty built the fane above!
آ آ آ آ Dost read my thought? Thy garb, alack, displays
آ آ آ آ Sore usage truly in each rent and stain,
آ آ آ آ Faugh! Wash though in Suburra! ï؟½Ware the dogs
آ آ آ آ Who may not so disdain a meal on thee!
آ آ آ آ What, stretchest forth a palm to catch my alms?
آ آ آ آ Aha, why yes: I must appear, who knows?
آ آ آ آ I, in my toga, to thy rags and thee,
آ آ آ آ Quï؟½stor, nay, ï؟½dile, Censor, Pol! perhaps
آ آ آ آ The very City-Prï؟½torï؟½s noble self!
آ آ آ آ As to me Cï؟½sar, so to thee am I?
آ آ آ آ Good: nor in vain shall prove thy quest, poor rogue!
آ آ آ آ Hither, hold palm out, take this quarter, as!ï؟½

آ آ آ آ And who did take it? As he raised his head,
آ آ آ آ (My gesture was a trifle, well, abrupt,)
آ آ آ آ Back fell the broad flap of the peasantï؟½s-hat,
آ آ آ آ The homespun cloak that muffled half his check
آ آ آ آ Dropped somewhat, and I had a glimpse, just one!
آ آ آ آ One was enough. Whose, whose might be the face?
آ آ آ آ That unkempt careless hair, brown, yellowish,
آ آ آ آ Those sparkling eyes beneath their eyebrowsï؟½ ridge
آ آ آ آ (Each meets each, and the hawk-nose rules between)
آ آ آ آ That was enough, no glimpse was needed more!
آ آ آ آ And terrifyingly into my mind
آ آ آ آ Came that quick-hushed report was whispered us,
آ آ آ آ ï؟½They do say, once a year in sordid garb
آ آ آ آ He plays the mendicant, sits all day long,
آ آ آ آ Asking and taking alms of who may pass,
آ آ آ آ And so averting, if submission help,
آ آ آ آ Fateï؟½s envy, the dread chance and change of things
آ آ آ آ When Fortune, for a word, a look, a naught,
آ آ آ آ Turns spiteful and, the petted lioness,
آ آ آ آ Strikes with her sudden paw, and prone falls each
آ آ آ آ Who patted late her neck superiorly,
آ آ آ آ Or trifled with those claw-tips velvet-sheathed.ï؟½
آ آ آ آ ï؟½Heï؟½s God!ï؟½ shouts Lucius Varius Rufus: ï؟½Man
آ آ آ آ And wormsï؟½-meat any moment!ï؟½ mutters low
آ آ آ آ Some Power, admonishing the mortal-born.
آ آ آ آ Ay, do you mind? Thereï؟½s meaning in the fact
آ آ آ آ That whoso conquers, triumphs, enters Rome,
آ آ آ آ Climbing the Capitolian, soaring thus
آ آ آ آ To gloryï؟½s summit, Publius, do you mark,
آ آ آ آ Ever the same attendant who, behind,
آ آ آ آ Above the Conquerorï؟½s head supports the crown
آ آ آ آ All-too-demonstrative for human wear,
آ آ آ آ One handï؟½s employment, all the while reserves
آ آ آ آ Its fellow, backward flung, to point how, close
آ آ آ آ Appended from the car, beneath the foot
آ آ آ آ Of the up-borne exulting Conqueror,
آ آ آ آ Frown, half-descried, the instruments of shame,
آ آ آ آ The malefactorï؟½s due. Crown, now, Cross, when?

آ آ آ آ Who stands secure? Are even Gods so safe?
آ آ آ آ Jupiter that just now is dominant,
آ آ آ آ Are not there ancient dismal tales how once
آ آ آ آ A predecessor reigned ere Saturn came,
آ آ آ آ And who can say if Jupiter be last?
آ آ آ آ Was it for nothing the gray Sibyl wrote
آ آ آ آ ï؟½Cï؟½sar Augustus regnant, shall be born
آ آ آ آ In blind Judï؟½a, one to master him,
آ آ آ آ Him and the universe? An old-wifeï؟½s tale?
آ آ آ آ Bath-drudge! Here, slave! No cheating! Our turn next.
آ آ آ آ No loitering, or be sure you taste the lash!
آ آ آ آ Two strigils, two oil-drippers, each a sponge!
meaning: About: Robert Browning was a British poet and playwright whose mastery of dramatic verse, especially dramatic monologues, made him one of the foremost Victorian poets.
